findings.summary: Parameters with underscore prefix (_action, _entity_state) in dispatch_wasm_integrations are immediately reassigned to non-prefixed names, suggesting the parameter names should be adjusted or the unused-variable suppression reconsidered
evidence: Line 195: _action: &str, and line 205: let action = _action.to_string();
Line 197: _entity_state: &EntityState, and line 207: let entity_state = _entity_state.clone();
findings.suggestion: Remove underscore prefixes from parameters that are actually used. The underscore prefix conventionally signals intentionally unused parameters, which is misleading here.

findings.summary: Helper functions env_bool and env_timeout in state/mod.rs use generic names that do not communicate their startup-only semantics
evidence: Line 93: fn env_bool(name: &str, default: bool) -> bool
Line 104: fn env_timeout() -> Duration
findings.suggestion: Consider renaming to startup_env_bool or init_env_bool to make the startup-only read semantics explicit, matching the determinism-ok annotations.

findings.summary: EntityActor::pre_start checks self.event_store twice in succession with redundant guard logic
evidence: Line 270: if let Some(ref store) = self.event_store { Self::replay_events(...) }
Line 276: if self.event_store.is_some() && state.events.is_empty() { ... }
Line 285: if let Some(ref store) = self.event_store { Self::persist_event(...) } -- this inner check is always true given the outer guard
findings.suggestion: The inner if-let at line 285 is redundant since it is nested inside a block where self.event_store.is_some() was already checked at line 276. Restructure to avoid the double check.

findings.summary: EntityActor::handle uses debug_assert! for preconditions then immediately checks the same condition with a runtime if-guard, creating dual enforcement paths
evidence: Lines 323-340: debug_assert! checks for states.contains, events.len < MAX, item_count <= MAX
Lines 343-356: if state.events.len() >= MAX_EVENTS_PER_ENTITY { return early } -- same check as the debug_assert above
findings.suggestion: This is intentional TigerStyle (assertions catch bugs in dev, runtime guards protect in release). However, only events.len() has the runtime fallback -- states.contains and item_count do not. Either add runtime guards for all three or document why only events.len() gets one.

findings.summary: State machine status values use bare String throughout EntityState, TransitionTable, and EntityEvent, losing compile-time guarantees about valid states
evidence: EntityState.status: String -- any string is accepted
TransitionTable.states: Vec<String> -- valid states are runtime-only knowledge
EntityEvent.from_status: String, to_status: String -- no compile-time constraint
findings.suggestion: This is a deliberate design choice for a spec-driven platform where entity types are defined at runtime. Consider a newtype wrapper (e.g., StateId(String)) with validation at construction boundaries to catch invalid state strings earlier.

findings.summary: ServerState::turso() panics when the event store is not configured or is not Turso, but the doc comment does not adequately warn callers about the precondition
evidence: Line 444: pub fn turso(&self) -> &temper_store_turso::TursoEventStore { self.persistent_store().expect(...) }
Doc comment says 'Panics if the event store is not configured' but callers may not realize this
findings.suggestion: Either remove turso() in favor of persistent_store() which returns Option, or add a # Panics section to the doc comment per Rust convention.

findings.summary: dispatch() returns Result<EntityResponse, String> while dispatch_tenant_action_core returns Result<EntityResponse, DispatchError>, losing structured error information at the boundary
evidence: Line 28: pub async fn dispatch(&self, cmd: DispatchCommand<'_>) -> Result<EntityResponse, String>
Line 40: .map_err(|e| e.to_string())? -- converts DispatchError to String
Line 146: pub(crate) async fn dispatch_tenant_action_core(...) -> Result<EntityResponse, DispatchError>
findings.suggestion: Propagate DispatchError through the public dispatch() API instead of flattening to String. The DispatchError enum was created specifically to preserve error context.

findings.summary: The codebase mixes Result<T, String> with typed thiserror enums at module boundaries, losing structured error information
evidence: DispatchError is defined with proper thiserror variants (ActorFailed, WasmFailed, AuthzDenied, Internal)
dispatch() converts DispatchError to String via .map_err(|e| e.to_string())
ServerState::with_specs returns Result<Self, String> instead of a typed error
register_tenant panics on error via .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("{e}"))
findings.suggestion: Standardize on typed errors at public API boundaries. The DispatchError enum is well-designed but underutilized because dispatch() flattens it back to String.

findings.summary: RwLock access uses two different patterns: .unwrap() with ci-ok annotation and .ok().and_then() silent swallowing
evidence: state/mod.rs line 403: registry.read().unwrap() -- panics on poison
state/dispatch/actions.rs line 46: reaction_dispatcher.read().ok().and_then(|slot| slot.clone()) -- silently ignores poison
state/dispatch/mod.rs line 116: denial_reason.lock().ok().and_then(...) -- silently ignores poison
Both patterns appear in production paths, not just tests
findings.suggestion: Pick one strategy: if lock poisoning is truly impossible (single-threaded actor), use .unwrap() consistently with ci-ok. If poisoning can happen, handle it consistently with Result propagation.

findings.summary: ServerState holds 30+ fields spanning actor management, telemetry, authorization, persistence, WASM, webhooks, evolution records, secrets, and configuration -- a wide config bag that crosses many domain boundaries
evidence: ServerState struct has 30+ pub fields covering: actor_system, csdl, entity_set_map, transition_tables, actor_registry, event_store, data_dir, agent_hints, authz, registry, entity_index, event_tx, metrics, record_store, pg_record_store, reaction_dispatcher, webhook_dispatcher, wasm_module_registry, wasm_engine, secrets_vault, and more
Constructor methods (new, from_registry, from_registry_shared) duplicate initialization of ~25 fields each
Methods are split across 6+ files via impl blocks in separate modules
findings.suggestion: Group related fields into sub-structs: PersistenceConfig (event_store, pg_record_store, data_dir), TelemetryConfig (event_tx, design_time_tx, metrics, agent_progress_tx), IntegrationConfig (wasm_engine, wasm_module_registry, webhook_dispatcher, reaction_dispatcher), AuthConfig (authz, secrets_vault, tenant_policies). This would reduce constructor duplication and clarify ownership boundaries.

findings.summary: The dispatch API has a three-layer wrapper chain: dispatch_action -> dispatch_tenant_action -> dispatch -> dispatch_tenant_action_core, where the intermediate layers add minimal value
evidence: dispatch_action (deprecated) calls dispatch_tenant_action
dispatch_tenant_action calls dispatch(DispatchCommand{..})
dispatch_tenant_action_ext calls dispatch(DispatchCommand{..})
dispatch calls dispatch_tenant_action_core and adds reaction cascade
Four public entry points for essentially one operation
findings.suggestion: Since dispatch_action is already deprecated, remove it and consider collapsing dispatch_tenant_action and dispatch_tenant_action_ext into direct callers of dispatch(DispatchCommand). The DispatchCommand struct was specifically designed to eliminate this wrapper chain.

findings.summary: TrajectoryEntry is constructed inline with 15 fields in multiple locations, creating repetitive boilerplate that could be reduced with builder methods
evidence: dispatch/actions.rs lines 158-175: inline TrajectoryEntry construction with 15 fields
dispatch/actions.rs lines 220-237: nearly identical TrajectoryEntry construction
dispatch/effects.rs lines 36-62: another inline TrajectoryEntry construction
odata/write.rs lines 75-92: yet another inline TrajectoryEntry construction
Each instance has the same pattern: most fields from ctx, a few varying
findings.suggestion: Add a TrajectoryEntry::from_dispatch(ctx, success, error) builder or From<PostDispatchContext> impl to eliminate the per-site boilerplate.

findings.summary: EntityResponse is constructed inline with 8 fields in 8+ locations within entity_actor/actor.rs, mostly with the same default pattern
evidence: Lines 344-356, 381-391, 450-458, 481-489, 493-501, 531-539, 542-551: all construct EntityResponse with custom_effects: vec![], scheduled_actions: vec![], spawn_requests: vec![], spec_governed: true
7 out of 8 constructions use the same defaults for 4 of the 8 fields
findings.suggestion: Add EntityResponse::success(state) and EntityResponse::failure(state, error) factory methods to eliminate the repetitive field initialization.

findings.summary: temper-server crate has grown to 13 root modules and 7+ subdirectories with ~15,000 lines, spanning HTTP routing, OData, entity actors, reactions, secrets, WASM, observation, and evolution -- broader scope than its stated role
evidence: lib.rs exports: api, authz, entity_actor, event_store, events, eventual_invariants, idempotency, observe, odata, reaction, registry, request_context, secrets, sentinel, state, wasm_registry, webhooks
Total production LOC in temper-server exceeds 15,000 across 60+ files
The crate description says 'HTTP server assembly' but includes business logic (entity_actor, reaction, evolution observation)
findings.suggestion: Consider extracting entity_actor and reaction into their own crates (e.g., temper-entity, temper-reaction) since they are core business logic rather than HTTP assembly. The observe subsystem could also be a separate feature-gated crate.

findings.summary: Post-dispatch effects (trajectory recording, SSE broadcast, cache update, webhook dispatch, WASM integration, spawn handling, scheduled actions) are orchestrated across multiple files with state.clone() passing
evidence: dispatch/actions.rs calls self.run_post_dispatch_effects(&ctx, response)
dispatch/effects.rs defines PostDispatchContext and 5+ effect handlers
dispatch/mod.rs line 201: state cloned for tokio::spawn background WASM dispatch
wasm.rs handles integration lookup, module resolution, and callback dispatch
findings.suggestion: The effect pipeline is well-structured but the handoff between dispatch_tenant_action_core and run_post_dispatch_effects could be clearer. Consider making PostDispatchContext carry the response mutably rather than returning a modified response.

findings.summary: ServerState has 6 constructor methods (new, with_specs, with_persistence, with_event_store, from_registry, from_registry_shared) that duplicate initialization of 25+ fields
evidence: new() initializes all 30+ fields at lines 196-257
from_registry_shared() duplicates the same initialization at lines 342-390
Broadcast channel creation duplicated 4 times in new() and again in from_registry_shared()
with_specs, with_persistence, with_event_store chain through new() so are less duplicated
findings.suggestion: Extract a default_channels() helper and a ServerStateBuilder to avoid duplicating field initialization across constructor paths.

findings.summary: EntityActor::handle method spans 250 lines with deeply nested match arms, mixing transition evaluation, persistence, telemetry emission, and error handling in a single method
evidence: handle() runs from line 301 to line 553 (~250 lines)
EntityMsg::Action arm alone is ~180 lines with 3 levels of nesting
Wide event construction duplicated in success (lines 402-414) and failure (lines 466-479) branches
EntityMsg::UpdateFields and EntityMsg::Delete have simple but repetitive EntityResponse construction
findings.suggestion: Extract the Action handler into a separate method (handle_action), and extract the wide event construction into a helper. The success/failure branches share significant structure that could be unified.

findings.summary: 44 production and test files exceed the project's own 500-line guideline, with the largest at 1,221 lines
evidence: mod_test.rs: 1221 lines, toml_parser.rs: 936 lines, smt.rs: 869 lines
odata/read.rs: 852 lines, actor.rs: 839 lines, sim_actor_system.rs: 760 lines
query_eval.rs: 746 lines, parser.rs: 731 lines
Project CLAUDE.md states: 'Files > 500 lines must be split into directory modules'
findings.suggestion: Prioritize splitting the largest files. toml_parser.rs could separate table/array/value parsing. actor.rs could extract the test suite. odata/read.rs could split entity vs collection read paths.

findings.summary: 22 files use HashMap/HashSet despite the deterministic simulation requirement for BTreeMap/BTreeSet in simulation-visible crates
evidence: temper-authz/src/engine.rs uses HashMap -- temper-authz is a dependency of temper-server (simulation-visible)
temper-authz/src/context.rs uses HashMap
temper-store-redis uses HashMap in mailbox.rs, placement.rs, cache.rs
temper-cli/src/util.rs uses HashMap (cli is not simulation-visible, so this is acceptable)
findings.suggestion: Audit HashMap usage in temper-authz and temper-store-redis. While these crates are not in the core simulation path, they are dependencies of temper-server. Consider converting to BTreeMap for consistency with the determinism policy.

findings.summary: ServerState has impl blocks split across 6+ files in the state/ directory, making it difficult to understand the full API surface
evidence: state/mod.rs: constructors and query methods
state/dispatch/actions.rs: dispatch, dispatch_action, dispatch_tenant_action, dispatch_tenant_action_ext, dispatch_tenant_action_core
state/dispatch/effects.rs: run_post_dispatch_effects, record_dispatch_trajectory, broadcast_state_change
state/dispatch/mod.rs: dispatch_wasm_integrations, wasm_authz_gate
state/entity_ops.rs: entity CRUD operations
state/persistence/: persistence methods
findings.suggestion: This is a known Rust pattern for organizing large impl blocks, but it makes the ServerState API surface opaque. Consider adding a summary doc comment in state/mod.rs listing all impl extension modules.

findings.summary: ServerState constructors read environment variables (TEMPER_XINV_ENFORCE, TEMPER_XINV_EVENTUAL_ENFORCE, TEMPER_ACTION_TIMEOUT_SECS) at construction time, coupling initialization to environment
evidence: Line 237: cross_invariant_enforce: env_bool("TEMPER_XINV_ENFORCE", true)
Line 238: cross_invariant_eventual_enforce: env_bool("TEMPER_XINV_EVENTUAL_ENFORCE", true)
Line 241: action_dispatch_timeout: env_timeout()
These are annotated with determinism-ok: read once at startup, which is the intended pattern
findings.suggestion: While the determinism-ok annotations are correct, consider accepting these values as constructor parameters or a Config struct to improve testability. The current approach requires env var manipulation in tests.

findings.summary: ServerState constructors automatically call register_builtin_wasm_modules() which compiles an embedded WASM binary, coupling server initialization to WASM compilation
evidence: Line 255: state.register_builtin_wasm_modules(); called in new()
Line 388: state.register_builtin_wasm_modules(); called in from_registry_shared()
Line 262: const HTTP_FETCH_WASM: &[u8] = include_bytes!(...) -- embeds ~200KB WASM binary
Line 265: self.wasm_engine.compile_and_cache(HTTP_FETCH_WASM) -- synchronous compilation in constructor
findings.suggestion: Make WASM registration lazy or opt-in. Tests that don't exercise WASM integration pay the compilation cost unnecessarily. Consider a with_builtin_wasm() builder method.

findings.summary: temper-cli/src/util.rs uses std::collections::HashMap while the rest of the codebase consistently uses BTreeMap
evidence: Line 3: use std::collections::HashMap;
Line 13: pub(crate) fn read_tla_sources(specs_dir: &Path) -> Result<HashMap<String, String>>
All other crates in the workspace use BTreeMap for ordered, deterministic collections
findings.suggestion: While the CLI is not simulation-visible, switching to BTreeMap would maintain codebase consistency. The CLI TLA+ source reading does not benefit from HashMap's performance characteristics.

findings.summary: Test files use inconsistent naming: some use #[path] attribute (router_test.rs, mod_test.rs, lib_tests.rs), others are in tests/ directories, others use #[cfg(test)] mod tests inline
evidence: router.rs uses #[path = "router_test.rs"] -- separate file via path attribute
observe/mod.rs uses #[path = "mod_test.rs"] -- same pattern
lib_tests.rs is a standalone file in temper-mcp
entity_actor/actor.rs has inline #[cfg(test)] mod tests
Integration tests live in crates/temper-server/tests/
findings.suggestion: The #[path] pattern is used for large test modules that would make the source file too long, which is reasonable. Document this as a convention: inline tests for <100 LOC of tests, #[path] for larger test suites.

findings.summary: temper-verify is correctly optional in temper-server (feature-gated), but temper-server still has a large dependency surface with 16 internal crate dependencies
evidence: temper-server depends on: temper-runtime, temper-odata, temper-spec, temper-jit, temper-store-postgres, temper-store-turso, temper-store-redis, temper-observe, temper-authz, temper-evolution, temper-wasm, temper-mcp, plus optional temper-verify and temper-store-sim
14 mandatory internal dependencies for one crate
temper-jit correctly does NOT depend on temper-verify (dep discipline maintained)
findings.suggestion: The temper-jit isolation is well-maintained. Consider whether temper-mcp dependency in temper-server is necessary or could be feature-gated, since MCP integration is a specialized capability.

findings.summary: Test strategy is strong with 430+ tests across 4 verification levels, but some crates (temper-observe, temper-optimize) have limited test coverage
evidence: temper-server has comprehensive unit + integration + DST tests (actor.rs, dst_lifecycle.rs, dst_multi_tenant.rs, dst_hotswap.rs, reaction_cascade.rs, wasm_dispatch.rs, gmail_oauth_dst.rs)
temper-platform has E2E DST tests (platform_e2e_dst.rs, system_entity_dst.rs)
temper-observe has 9 source files but tests appear limited to clickhouse.rs and trajectory.rs
temper-optimize has 6 source files with #[cfg(test)] in placement.rs, safety.rs, but query.rs and types.rs appear untested
findings.suggestion: Add tests for the wide_event emission pipeline in temper-observe and for query optimization logic in temper-optimize. These are runtime-critical paths.

findings.summary: dispatch_wasm_integrations takes 9 positional parameters, and dispatch_tenant_action_core takes 8, despite the project having a DispatchCommand struct designed to replace this pattern
evidence: dispatch_wasm_integrations at line 190: 9 parameters (self, tenant, entity_type, entity_id, _action, custom_effects, _entity_state, agent_ctx, action_params)
dispatch_tenant_action_core at line 146: 8 parameters (self, tenant, entity_type, entity_id, action, params, agent_ctx, await_integration)
#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)] used to suppress the lint
WasmDispatchRequest struct exists at line 34 but dispatch_wasm_integrations doesn't use it in its public signature
findings.suggestion: dispatch_wasm_integrations should accept WasmDispatchRequest instead of individual parameters -- the struct already exists. dispatch_tenant_action_core should accept DispatchCommand instead of 7 positional parameters.

findings.summary: The dispatch family returns different error types: dispatch() returns Result<_, String>, dispatch_tenant_action() returns Result<_, String>, but dispatch_tenant_action_core returns Result<_, DispatchError>
evidence: Line 28: dispatch() -> Result<EntityResponse, String>
Line 106: dispatch_tenant_action() -> Result<EntityResponse, String>
Line 155: dispatch_tenant_action_core() -> Result<EntityResponse, DispatchError>
findings.suggestion: All public dispatch methods should return Result<EntityResponse, DispatchError> for consistency. The String flattening in dispatch() defeats the purpose of the typed error enum.

findings.summary: Some observe endpoints check authorization via require_observe_auth while others appear to rely on the router-level middleware without explicit checks
evidence: require_observe_auth() defined in authz/helpers.rs checks Cedar policies for observe endpoints
Admin and System principals bypass checks (lines 54-58)
The observe router has 20+ endpoints but the require_observe_auth pattern must be called explicitly in each handler
No middleware-level auth enforcement for the /observe prefix
findings.suggestion: Consider adding a middleware layer for the /observe router that enforces authorization uniformly, rather than relying on each handler to call require_observe_auth. This prevents accidental omissions.

findings.summary: CORS is configured with allow_origin(Any) which permits requests from any domain, potentially exposing the API to CSRF-style attacks in browser contexts
evidence: Line 74: .allow_origin(Any) in CorsLayer configuration
findings.suggestion: For development this is acceptable, but production deployments should restrict CORS origins. Consider making CORS origins configurable via environment variable or server configuration.

findings.summary: TLA+ specification support persists in production code paths despite being documented as legacy in favor of I/O Automaton TOML
evidence: temper-spec/src/lib.rs line 17: pub mod tlaplus; -- TLA+ module is still public
temper-cli/src/util.rs: read_tla_sources() reads .tla files from disk
CLAUDE.md states: 'TLA+ is legacy -- from_tla_source() is #[cfg(test)] only'
temper-spec/src/tlaplus/extractor.rs is 664 lines of production TLA+ parsing code
temper-codegen/src/generator.rs and state_machine.rs still reference TLA+
15 files still reference TLA+ patterns
findings.suggestion: The TLA+ code is well-contained behind the tlaplus module boundary. To complete the migration, gate the tlaplus module behind a cfg(test) or legacy feature flag, and update the CLI to remove read_tla_sources.

findings.summary: The in-memory RecordStore in temper-evolution is deprecated (ADR-0025) but still actively used by ServerState and PlatformState
evidence: store.rs module doc: 'Deprecated: Evolution records are now managed as IOA entities'
store.rs has #[allow(deprecated)] on multiple items
state/mod.rs line 24: #[allow(deprecated)] // ADR-0025 Phase 4: remove after sentinel/insight dispatch migrated
state/mod.rs line 148: pub record_store: Arc<RecordStore>, -- still a field
platform/state.rs line 11: #[allow(deprecated)] // ADR-0025 Phase 4
findings.suggestion: The ADR-0025 Phase 4 migration is tracked but not complete. The deprecated annotations with migration notes are good practice. Prioritize completing the migration to remove the in-memory store.

findings.summary: temper-server/src/ root directory has 13 files mixing concerns: HTTP infrastructure (router.rs, response.rs, request_context.rs), business logic (query_eval.rs, sentinel.rs, eventual_invariants.rs), and plumbing (event_store.rs, wasm_registry.rs)
evidence: Root files: event_store.rs, events.rs, eventual_invariants.rs, idempotency.rs, lib.rs, query_eval.rs, registry_bootstrap.rs, request_context.rs, response.rs, router.rs, router_test.rs, sentinel.rs, wasm_registry.rs
13 files at root level with mixed concerns
Some have clear subdirectory candidates: sentinel.rs could join observe/, wasm_registry.rs could join a wasm/ subdirectory
findings.suggestion: Move sentinel.rs into observe/ (it generates observation records). Move wasm_registry.rs into a wasm/ subdirectory alongside wasm-related dispatch code. Move eventual_invariants.rs into a constraints/ or invariants/ subdirectory.

findings.summary: Reference apps (ecommerce, oncall) are workspace members at the top level, which is clean, but they duplicate test infrastructure patterns
evidence: Workspace members include reference-apps/ecommerce and reference-apps/oncall
Both have DST test files (ecommerce_dst.rs, oncall_dst.rs) with similar boilerplate
Config bag mentions: 36 each in both DST test files
findings.suggestion: Consider extracting shared DST test harness infrastructure from the reference apps into a test-utils crate or shared module to reduce duplication.

findings.summary: EntityState tracks item_count as a dedicated field AND in the counters BTreeMap, creating two sources of truth for the same concept
evidence: types.rs line 52: pub item_count: usize, -- dedicated field
types.rs line 54: pub counters: BTreeMap<String, usize>, -- generic counter map
The doc comment on item_count says: 'legacy -- prefer counters["items"] for new code'
effects.rs line 63: ctx.counters.insert("items".to_string(), state.item_count); -- syncs item_count into counters
findings.suggestion: Complete the migration from item_count to counters["items"]. The sync in build_eval_context creates a coupling point. Either deprecate item_count with a migration path or remove the counters["items"] alias.

findings.summary: Multiple functions across the codebase take 7-9 positional parameters despite available struct types that could replace them
evidence: dispatch_wasm_integrations: 9 parameters with #[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]
dispatch_tenant_action_core: 8 parameters with #[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]
persist_evolution_record: 7 parameters (record_id, record_type, status, created_by, derived_from, data_json)
52 wide_param_bag locations identified in the blind packet analysis
findings.suggestion: The DispatchCommand and WasmDispatchRequest structs show the correct pattern -- extend it to internal methods. For persist_evolution_record, consider an EvolutionRecordInput struct.
